mechanism: Study-test cycle with competing item inhibition: encode list items, practice selective retrieval of subset creating competition, measure post-retrieval accessibility of unpracticed items. Storage strength (permanent learning trace) and retrieval strength (current accessibility) dissociate; inhibition operates on retrieval strength via competitive suppression during retrieval practice.

Discrimination learning via attentional differentiation: expose to two similar stimuli in intermixed or blocked sequences, encode stimulus features with variable attention distribution, measure subsequent perceptual discrimination via same-different judgments. Intermixing promotes feature comparison and unique feature salience; massing produces habituation of distinctive features.

Serial and free recall with auditory distraction: present visual list for memory, present concurrent irrelevant auditory stream, retrieve items via serial or free order, measure recall accuracy and intrusion errors. Category-cueing processes active in free recall become vulnerable to phonologically related distractors; serial recall relies on order codes unaffected by phonological content.

Lexical selection via response buffer control: present picture and written distractor simultaneously or with 1000ms delay, measure naming latency across immediate and delayed response conditions and word frequency manipulations. Semantic interference persists in delayed naming despite eliminated frequency effect, indicating postlexical locus via exclusion of competing output representations from single-channel response buffer.

Reading pathway selection via context-dependent control: read words/nonwords in predictable alternating sequence, measure response times on switch versus stay trials as index of pathway reconfiguration. Relative emphasis of lexical versus nonlexical routes adjusted based on stimulus demands; exogenous control linked to stimulus onset rather than endogenous preparation.

Semantic attractor dynamics with graded similarity: represent concepts as feature vectors, compute near neighbors (high feature overlap) and distant neighbors (low overlap), measure word recognition latency and model settling patterns. Distant neighbors provide gradient facilitating attractor convergence; near neighbors create conflicting subbasins increasing saddle-point probability and slowing settling.
